medications.indication_rationale: Antiplatelet therapy is inappropriate and potentially dangerous in suspected aortic dissection due to bleeding risk.

medications.drug_name: any self-directed extra dose of blood pressure medication
medications.indication_rationale: Blood pressure in suspected dissection with limb malperfusion must be carefully titrated intravenously by paramedics/ED to balance reducing aortic stress against maintaining limb perfusion; self-dosing is unsafe.
contraindications_in_this_case: Suspected aortic dissection with limb malperfusion

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Remain still, ideally lying down, and avoid walking or exertion despite the leg symptoms, until paramedics arrive.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Movement increases aortic wall shear stress and could worsen the dissection; walking on an ischaemic leg also risks further injury.

patient_education_points.point: Your symptoms — sudden tearing back pain spreading to your belly and side, along with a cold, numb leg and a history of high blood pressure — raise serious concern for a tear in your main artery (aortic dissection) affecting blood flow to your leg. This is a life-threatening emergency.
Call 000 for an ambulance right now if you haven't already.
Do not take aspirin, and do not take an extra blood pressure tablet — blood pressure needs to be carefully managed by the paramedics with medication given directly into a vein.
Try to stay still and avoid walking on the affected leg until help arrives.

follow_up_plan.interval: Immediate emergency transport and hospital admission for emergency vascular/cardiothoracic surgical evaluation
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: CT aortogram findings, limb viability and need for revascularisation, blood pressure control, and long-term hypertension and smoking cessation management
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Not applicable — already an emergency requiring immediate transport

must_include_items: Explicit instruction NOT to take aspirin or extra blood pressure medication
Instruction to remain still and avoid walking on the affected leg
Recognition of the leg symptoms as a limb malperfusion emergency, not a separate minor problem
errors_of_omission: No explicit warning against aspirin/self-dosed antihypertensives
No recognition of the leg ischaemia as part of the same emergency
errors_of_commission: Attributing the leg symptoms to a separate, non-urgent cause (e.g. sciatica)
Suggesting the patient try to walk it off or wait to see if it improves
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Failing to connect the leg ischaemia to the back pain as a single vascular emergency, or recommending aspirin, are critical safety failures.
